title: Enhancement of superconductivity by polarization of magnetic impurities in disordered films
authors: Gleb S. Seleznev, Yakov V. Fominov
abstract: Dirty superconducting films with magnetic impurities can exhibit nontrivial behavior in a magnetic field that polarizes the impurity spins. As predicted by Kharitonov and Feigelman (KF) [JETP Lett. 82, 421 (2005)], this polarization reduces the exchange scattering rate. Consequently, a parallel magnetic field can enhance the critical temperature $T_c$ when magnetic-field pair breaking is weak, as realized for strong spin-orbit scattering and small film thickness. Recently, Llanos et al. [Nat. Phys. (2026)] observed a pronounced enhancement of $T_c$ consistent with the KF theory. The same experiment also reported an enhancement of the perpendicular upper critical field $H_{c2}^{\perp}$ and a suppression of the London penetration depth $λ_L$ by a parallel magnetic field. These quantities were not considered in the original KF theory. To address this gap, we develop a theoretical framework based on Gor'kov's diagrammatic technique for dirty superconductors. We extend the KF theory in two experimentally relevant directions: (i) to arbitrary temperatures $T<T_c$ and several superconducting observables, and (ii) to arbitrary magnetic-field orientations. As a result, we demonstrate theoretically the suppression of $λ_L$ and the enhancement of $H_{c2}^{\perp}$ by a parallel magnetic field, in agreement with experiment.
summary: This paper extends the theory of superconductivity in disordered films to show how a parallel magnetic field can enhance superconductivity by polarizing magnetic impurities. By reducing spin-flip scattering, the field can increase the critical temperature, increase the upper critical field, and even restore superconductivity in previously normal films.
main_problem: The paper aims to extend the Kharitonov and Feigelman (KF) theory to explain recent experimental observations of superconductivity enhancement, specifically addressing the enhancement of the perpendicular upper critical field and the suppression of the London penetration depth by a parallel magnetic field.
main_result: The authors theoretically demonstrate that a parallel magnetic field suppresses the London penetration depth and enhances the perpendicular upper critical field by polarizing magnetic impurities and reducing the effective exchange scattering rate. They also predict a gapless-to-gapped transition and the possibility of field-induced superconductivity restoration.
method: The study utilizes Gor'kov's diagrammatic technique for dirty superconductors within the Matsubara representation, employing the self-consistent Born approximation and Dyson's equation to derive superconducting observables.
model_or_system: The system consists of dirty (disordered) superconducting thin films containing potential, spin-orbit, and magnetic impurities. The model uses a Bogoliubov-de Gennes Hamiltonian that accounts for orbital and paramagnetic effects from external magnetic fields.
key_observables: Critical temperature (Tc), superconducting order parameter (Delta), superfluid density (nsc), London penetration depth (lambda_L), perpendicular upper critical field (Hc2_perp), and spectral gap (Eg).
important_parameters: Exchange scattering rates (nu_s, nu_inf), spin-orbit scattering rate (nu_so), magnetic field components (h_parallel, h_perp), impurity spin (S), and film thickness (d).
main_assumptions: The films are in the dirty limit; the model assumes strong spin-orbit scattering to suppress paramagnetic effects; the Kondo effect is neglected (T > TK); and impurities are assumed to be independent and uniformly distributed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of a disordered film with different impurity types; Figure 2 displays self-energy diagrams for various scattering processes; Figure 4 shows numerical plots of nonmonotonic Tc, nsc, and Hc2_perp; Figure 5 compares theoretical curves with experimental data for Tc, Hc2_perp, and lambda_L.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the formulation of the Hamiltonian and the physical model, followed by the development of the theoretical framework using Gor'kov's technique. It then explores specific limits (unpolarized and fully polarized), analyzes the gapless-to-gapped transition, and concludes with numerical results and comparison to experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Disordered superconducting film of thickness d con- taining potential (black balls), spin-orbit (blue balls), and magnetic (red balls) impurities with spin S. The correspond- ing scattering rates are ν0, νso, and νs. The film is subjected to an external magnetic field H, which polarizes the magnetic impurities. We use energy units, h = µBH, where µB is the Bohr magneton. The field can have an arbitrary orientation, and the quantization axis z is aligned with it.

FIG. 2. Self-energy diagrams in the self-consistent Born approximation, calculated using the exact Green’s function ˆGqs(εn, p) from Eq. (19). The diagrams describe scattering by potential impurities (scattering rate ν0), spin-orbit impurities (scattering rate νso), and magnetic impurities (scattering rate νs). Exchange scattering by magnetic impurities is divided into two groups: processes without and with spin flips. The impurity lines in the top row, which describe potential scattering, spin-orbit scattering, and exchange scattering without spin flips (proportional to Lzz), carry zero Matsubara frequency, ωm = 0. The impurity lines in the bottom row, which describe spin-flip processes...

FIG. 3. Phase diagrams of the superconducting film for different exchange scattering rates νs and impurity spins: (a) S < S0 ≈10.3 and (b) S > S0. The paramagnetic and orbital effects of the magnetic field are neglected. The gapless-to-gapped transition can only be observed in the range νgap s < νs < νgap ∞, and magnetic-field-induced restoration of superconductivity (SC) is observed in the range νcr s < νs < νcr ∞.

FIG. 4. Nonmonotonic dependence of superconducting observables on the parallel magnetic field h∥: (a), (d), and (g) critical temperature Tc; (b), (e), and (h) superfluid density nsc at T = 0; and (c), (f), and (i) perpendicular upper critical field h⊥ c2 at T = 0. The superfluid density is normalized to nsc0, its value at h∥= 0 and νs = 0. The nonmonotonic curves have maxima at finite fields, marked by black dots, and arise from competition between the polarization and pair-breaking effects of the magnetic field. All panels are calculated for S = 1/2, gs = 2, ν0 = 104Tc0, νso = 103Tc0, ζs = 5, pF d = 30, pF l = 5, and different exchange scattering rates νs. Panels (a), (b), and (c)...

FIG. 5. Comparison between the experimental data of Ref. [18] (dots) and the corresponding theoretical curves (solid and dashed lines) as functions of H∥for (a) the critical temperature Tc, (b) the perpendicular upper critical field H⊥ c2, and (c) the London penetration depth λL. For all theoretical curves, we use S = 5/2, gs = 2, ν0 = 1032Tc0, νso = 348Tc0, νs = 0.89Tc0, ζs = 1.25, pF d = 20, and Tc0 = 717 mK. These values correspond to the experimental estimates presented in Table S4 of Ref. [18].
